text:
This study examines the reactive partitioning of cis- and trans-IEPOX onto hydrated inorganic seed surfaces in the absence of additional strong acid. Under the conditions examined, no OA forms on dry ammonium sulfate (AS) seed, but is quickly and strongly produced on AS seeds with relatively moderate liquid water content between 40–75% (w/w). This appears to be a kinetically limited process as growth to maximum value was observed for as long as IEPOX was available in the gas phase. Finally, there were essentially no differences between the cis- and trans-IEPOX isomers in terms of total yield, composition or retention in the particle phase.

Although reactive uptake onto hydrated AS seeds treated with NaOH led to substantial OA loadings, these levels were not highly dependent on pH, suggesting that the pH dependence for IEPOX-derived OA formed on ammonium-containing particles is not high. Upon drying, there was little remaining OA mass when sodium was substituted for ammonium in the seed particles. The Henry’s Law coefficient of IEPOX adsorbed onto NaCl particles at an ionic strength of 9 M (adjusted to match conditions of seeds made from 10 wt % ammonium sulfate solution in 50 mM H2SO4) was found to be 3 × 10⁷ M atm⁻¹ (range of −50 / +100%). Replacing sulfate with chloride in the seeds of ammonium sulfate particles had a pronounced effect on OA yields.

Online aerosol mass spectrometry (AMS) and offline filter analysis were used to identify complex organic aerosol (OA) composition over rural/forested areas, which included oxygenated hydrocarbons, organosulfates, and other lesser identified species, including amines. Understanding these observations can help explain the observations of low correlation between IEPOX-derived OA and particle acidity/liquid water in humid/ammonium-rich conditions; specifically that this system is limited by availability of particular nucleophiles rather than catalyst activity or availability of water.
explanation: Contains detailed, plausible chemical experimental descriptions and numbers; this level of technical nuance is typical of human-written science text, though it cannot rule out AI authorship.
